basic_info.name: Morpheus (Decentralized AI Network)
basic_info.status: Production (Morpheus mainnet launched on Arbitrum in 2024; Lumerin Protocol integrated for decentralized AI routing; MOR token live with staking and rewards; compute marketplace operational; ecosystem expanding with multiple Smart Agent implementations; MOR token listed on DEXes)
basic_info.ecosystem: Arbitrum (primary L2 for MOR token and smart contracts); Ethereum (for stETH capital deposits and governance); Lumerin Protocol (decentralized routing layer); expanding to other chains. The Morpheus ecosystem is built on Arbitrum for low-cost, high-throughput operations with capital contracts on Ethereum mainnet.
basic_info.launch_date: 2024 (Morpheus whitepaper published late 2023 by anonymous authors inspired by the Techno Capital Machine concept; MOR token fair launch in early-mid 2024 on Arbitrum; Lumerin routing integration 2024; compute marketplace operational by mid-2024; ongoing development through decentralized contributor community) [uncertain]

onboarding_flow.registration_steps: 1) Obtain an Ethereum/Arbitrum wallet (MetaMask or compatible). This wallet serves as the agent's primary identity. 2) For Smart Agent development: use the Morpheus Smart Agent framework (open-source) to build an AI agent. The framework supports LLM integration, tool use, and blockchain interactions. 3) For compute providers: register as a compute provider by staking MOR tokens on the Morpheus compute smart contract on Arbitrum. Configure the compute node to serve inference requests. 4) For capital providers: deposit stETH (Lido staked ETH) into the Morpheus Capital smart contract on Ethereum mainnet. Yield from stETH is used to purchase and distribute MOR rewards. 5) For code contributors: submit code contributions to the Morpheus GitHub repositories. Contributions are reviewed and weighted for MOR reward distribution. 6) Smart Agents connect to the Morpheus network via the Lumerin routing protocol: the agent registers its capabilities and endpoint with the decentralized router. 7) The Lumerin Protocol routes user/agent requests to available compute providers based on provider availability, stake weight, and pricing. 8) Agents can access LLM inference through the decentralized compute marketplace without centralized API keys. 9) Payment for compute is handled via MOR tokens or crypto payments through the routing layer. 10) The agent is now operational on the Morpheus network, discoverable through the Lumerin router, and can interact with other agents and users.
onboarding_flow.human_involvement: full-setup (human must create wallet, deploy/configure the Smart Agent, stake tokens for compute/capital roles; initial agent code development requires human; the Morpheus community is permissionless but all initial setup steps require human action; after deployment, agents operate autonomously)
onboarding_flow.agent_autonomy: High autonomy after setup. Smart Agents can autonomously: interact with LLMs via decentralized compute, execute blockchain transactions using their wallet, discover and communicate with other agents via Lumerin routing, and process user requests. The permissionless nature means any agent with a wallet and MOR tokens can participate without approval. Agents can self-select compute providers, manage their own token balances, and operate continuously. Initial wallet creation, staking, and code deployment require human action.
onboarding_flow.time_to_first_action: minutes to hours (wallet creation is instant; MOR token acquisition requires DEX trading or earning through contributions; compute provider registration requires staking transaction on Arbitrum which confirms in seconds; Smart Agent deployment and Lumerin routing registration takes minutes; overall time depends primarily on MOR token acquisition)

identity_model.identity_type: Wallet (Ethereum/Arbitrum wallet address). The agent's primary identity is its blockchain wallet address (secp256k1 ECDSA key pair). This wallet is used for: staking, receiving MOR rewards, paying for compute, signing transactions, and authenticating with the Lumerin routing protocol. There is no separate agent-specific identity token (like ERC-721) -- the wallet address IS the identity. [uncertain]
identity_model.key_management: Standard Ethereum wallet key management. (1) Private key generated locally and stored by the agent or its operator (MetaMask, hardware wallet, or programmatic key management). (2) No protocol-level key management service -- agents are responsible for their own key security. (3) For autonomous agents: private keys must be accessible to the agent runtime for signing transactions, creating a hot wallet security consideration. (4) Key rotation: changing wallet address changes the agent's identity; stake and reputation history do not transfer to a new address. (5) No built-in HSM, MPC, or threshold signing at the protocol level. (6) Agents could integrate with external key management solutions (Lit Protocol, Phala TEE, etc.) for enhanced key security. [uncertain]
identity_model.multi_instance_support: Not natively supported at the protocol level. A single wallet address represents a single identity. Multiple runtime instances could share the same wallet private key (risky), but the protocol does not provide multi-instance coordination or key derivation for the same identity. Multiple separate agents with different wallets can be operated by the same entity. [uncertain]
identity_model.recovery_mechanism: Standard Ethereum wallet recovery (seed phrase / mnemonic). If the private key or seed phrase is lost, the wallet (and all associated MOR tokens, staking positions, and identity history) is irrecoverable. No protocol-level social recovery, backup key, or account abstraction. The decentralized and permissionless nature means there is no authority that can restore access. Smart contract positions (staking, capital deposits) remain locked and inaccessible if keys are lost. [uncertain]
identity_model.versioning_support: No formal on-chain versioning for agent code or model versions. The Morpheus community tracks code contributions via GitHub (off-chain). Smart Agent implementations can be versioned by their developers using standard software versioning practices. The Lumerin routing layer does not maintain version metadata for registered agents. Compute provider models/capabilities are self-reported without on-chain version tracking. [uncertain]

security.authentication_method: Wallet signature (Ethereum secp256k1 ECDSA signatures for all on-chain transactions including staking, compute requests, and governance); Lumerin routing authentication uses wallet-based identity verification; crypto payment verification provides implicit authentication (only the wallet holder can authorize payments)
security.known_vulnerabilities: 1) Hot wallet risk: autonomous Smart Agents require access to private keys for signing, creating risk of key theft if the agent's runtime environment is compromised. 2) Lumerin routing centralization risk: while designed to be decentralized, the routing protocol's current implementation and validator set may have centralization concerns in early stages. 3) MOR token economics: the reward distribution mechanism (capital, code, compute, community) could be gamed by well-funded actors concentrating stETH deposits to dominate capital rewards. 4) Compute provider quality: no formal verification that compute providers are running the claimed models or producing accurate outputs -- a provider could serve lower-quality inference to reduce costs. 5) Smart contract risks: MOR distribution contracts handle significant value (stETH deposits); bugs could lead to fund loss. 6) Fair launch vulnerability: early MOR distribution concentrated among initial participants before broader awareness. 7) Anonymous team: the founding contributors are anonymous, which while aligned with decentralization principles, creates accountability concerns. [uncertain]
security.revocation_speed: minutes (Arbitrum L2 transaction confirmation for unstaking or withdrawal requests; however, some staking positions may have lock-up periods or unbonding delays; wallet-level key compromise requires manual response to move funds or unstake; no instant kill switch at the protocol level) [uncertain]
security.anti_sybil: staking (MOR token staking required for compute providers creates economic barrier; stETH capital deposits for capital providers represent significant financial commitment); fee (Arbitrum gas fees for transactions, though very low); stake-weighted reputation (higher-staked compute providers receive more routing traffic, making meaningful participation expensive). However, creating multiple wallet addresses is trivially easy with no per-identity cost beyond gas fees, making basic Sybil attacks at the wallet level inexpensive. The economic barriers are at the staking/capital layer, not the identity layer. [uncertain]
security.data_exposure_risk: Wallet addresses and transaction history are publicly visible on Arbitrum/Ethereum. Staking amounts, MOR token balances, and capital deposit sizes are transparent on-chain. Compute requests routed through Lumerin may expose request content to the compute provider (the provider must process the inference request). Smart Agent source code on GitHub is public. No PII is required for participation. Agent operational details (which models, what tasks) may be observable through transaction patterns.
security.bot_to_bot_attack_surface: The Morpheus protocol does not provide built-in protections against agent-to-agent prompt injection, social engineering, or influence operations. Smart Agents interact through the Lumerin routing layer, which handles routing but not content validation or semantic security. Compute providers can potentially observe and manipulate inference requests/responses. The permissionless nature means any agent can attempt to interact with any other agent. Application-layer security is the responsibility of individual Smart Agent developers. The community has discussed but not formally implemented agent-to-agent trust frameworks. [uncertain]
security.skill_plugin_vetting: No formal vetting or sandboxing. Smart Agents are built using the open-source Morpheus framework, and developers can include any skills, tools, or plugins they choose. There is no centralized marketplace, approval process, or automated security scanning for agent capabilities. Code contributions to the core Morpheus repositories go through community review on GitHub, but individual Smart Agent deployments are unvetted. The permissionless design philosophy explicitly avoids gatekeeping. [uncertain]
security.attestation_mechanism: No TEE attestation, Verifiable Credentials, or formal code hash verification at the protocol level. Agent integrity is not cryptographically attested. Staking serves as economic attestation (skin in the game) but does not prove code integrity or model correctness. Compute provider quality is implicitly validated through market feedback (users stop using poor providers) rather than cryptographic attestation. [uncertain]

offchain_onchain_linking.linking_support: yes (native -- the Morpheus architecture links on-chain identity (wallet), on-chain economics (staking/rewards), and off-chain compute (LLM inference via Lumerin routing))
offchain_onchain_linking.linking_mechanism: Wallet-based linking: the same Ethereum wallet address serves as both the on-chain identity (for staking, receiving rewards, governance participation on Arbitrum/Ethereum) and the off-chain identity (for authenticating with Lumerin routing, receiving compute requests, and operating the Smart Agent). Lumerin routing maps wallet addresses to off-chain compute endpoints. Staking transactions on-chain link the wallet to the compute provider's off-chain infrastructure. Capital deposits on Ethereum mainnet link to reward distribution on Arbitrum.
offchain_onchain_linking.transfer_behavior: N/A -- agent identity is wallet-based, not token-based. There is no agent identity NFT to transfer. MOR tokens can be freely transferred between wallets, but staking positions and compute provider registrations are tied to the wallet that created them. Transferring identity would require sharing the private key (insecure). Capital deposits (stETH) can be withdrawn by the depositor's wallet. Reputation and contribution history are permanently tied to the wallet address and do not transfer. [uncertain]

delegation_and_authorization.delegation_model: No formal delegation protocol. The Morpheus ecosystem relies on direct wallet-based authorization. (1) Human-to-agent: the human provides the wallet private key or deploys the agent with wallet access -- direct key sharing rather than scoped delegation. (2) Agent-to-agent: Smart Agents interact via Lumerin routing with standard crypto payment authorization -- no delegation chains or authorization tokens. (3) Compute requests are authorized by payment (the requester pays MOR for inference). (4) Governance participation (MOR token voting) is direct wallet-based. No OAuth OBO, OIDC-A, or AP2 mandate-based delegation. [uncertain]
delegation_and_authorization.authorization_granularity: Coarse (role/scope). The protocol defines broad roles: capital provider, code contributor, compute provider, and community/user. Within each role, authorization is binary (staked or not). No fine-grained ABAC, parameter-level access control, or intent-aware mandates at the protocol level. Compute access is pay-per-request without usage restrictions beyond economic cost. Smart Agents can implement application-level authorization internally.

discovery_and_interoperability.discovery_mechanism: Lumerin routing protocol (decentralized routing layer that matches compute requests with available providers; agents and compute providers register their endpoints and capabilities with Lumerin routers; discovery is handled through the P2P routing network rather than a static registry). On-chain: staking positions and MOR holder addresses are queryable on Arbitrum, but this provides economic identity rather than capability-based discovery. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.capability_declaration: Compute providers self-report their capabilities (supported models, hardware specs, pricing) through Lumerin routing registration. Smart Agents declare their capabilities informally through their user interfaces or documentation. No formal Agent Card, JSON-LD manifest, or standardized capability declaration schema at the protocol level. The Lumerin routing protocol handles matching requests to providers based on declared capabilities, but the declaration format is specific to the Lumerin implementation. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.cross_platform_portability: Partially portable. Wallet-based identity is portable across any EVM-compatible chain. MOR tokens are on Arbitrum, limiting direct economic participation to that chain. Smart Agent code is open-source and can be run anywhere, but integration with the Morpheus compute marketplace requires Lumerin routing connectivity. The decentralized routing approach avoids platform lock-in for the agent runtime. Cross-chain bridges could extend MOR token utility. No W3C DID or universal portable identity layer. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.standards_compliance: ERC-20 (MOR token on Arbitrum); Ethereum secp256k1 (wallet cryptography); Lumerin Protocol (custom routing standard, originally developed for hashrate marketplace). No explicit W3C DID, W3C VC, OIDC, SPIFFE, OAuth 2.0, ERC-721, ERC-8004, A2A, or MCP compliance. The Morpheus architecture follows crypto-native standards (EVM, token standards) rather than enterprise or web identity standards. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.protocol_composability: Moderate. (1) MOR token integrates with Arbitrum DeFi ecosystem (DEXes, lending). (2) stETH capital deposits compose with Lido/Ethereum staking. (3) Smart Agents can interact with any on-chain protocol via their wallet. (4) Lumerin routing could theoretically route requests to/from agents on other platforms. (5) The open-source Smart Agent framework can integrate external tools and APIs. (6) No direct composability with A2A, MCP, ERC-8004, AP2, or x402 at the protocol level, though individual agents could implement these. The Techno Capital Machine model is conceptually composable -- other projects have forked the MOR reward distribution approach. [uncertain]

push_pull_communication.push_support: P2P messaging via Lumerin routing (compute requests and responses are pushed through the decentralized routing network); smart contract events (MOR distribution events, staking events on Arbitrum emitted as EVM events); no built-in WebSocket, SSE, or webhook push notification system for agent-to-agent communication. [uncertain]
push_pull_communication.pull_support: On-chain reads (Arbitrum/Ethereum RPC for querying MOR balances, staking positions, reward accruals); Lumerin routing queries (discover available compute providers); REST API (individual Smart Agents may expose their own HTTP APIs); blockchain indexers for historical data
push_pull_communication.subscription_model: Smart contract events (standard EVM event subscriptions on Arbitrum for MOR distribution, staking changes, and governance events); Lumerin routing protocol handles real-time request-response matching but does not provide a general-purpose pub/sub system; no built-in token-based or channel-based subscription model. [uncertain]

trust_and_reputation.trust_model: Decentralized (economic/stake-weighted reputation). Trust is established through: (1) MOR token staking by compute providers (economic commitment), (2) stETH capital deposits by capital providers (significant financial commitment), (3) Code contribution history tracked on GitHub and weighted for rewards, (4) Market-based trust -- users gravitate toward reliable compute providers based on experience, (5) No centralized trust authority; the permissionless design relies on economic incentives rather than credential verification.
trust_and_reputation.reputation_system: Stake-weighted reputation. Compute providers with more MOR staked receive proportionally more routing traffic through Lumerin, creating a de facto reputation system based on economic commitment. Capital providers' contribution is measured by stETH deposited and MOR earned. Code contributors' reputation is tracked through the community's contribution weighting system (GitHub-based). No formal on-chain reputation registry (like ERC-8004), peer review system, or Verifiable Credential-based reputation. The approximately $20M in MOR rewards distributed across four pools (capital, code, compute, community) serves as the primary incentive alignment mechanism. [uncertain]

payment_and_economics.payment_integration: Native token (MOR token on Arbitrum for compute payments, staking, and governance); crypto payments (ETH/stETH for capital deposits on Ethereum mainnet); the Lumerin routing protocol handles payment routing between compute requesters and providers; MOR token tradeable on DEXes for liquidity. No integration with AP2, ACP, x402, or fiat payment protocols at the protocol level.
payment_and_economics.economic_model: Techno Capital Machine (TCM) model with four reward pools. (1) Capital providers: deposit stETH on Ethereum mainnet; yield is used to buy MOR on market, distributed proportionally to depositors. ~24% of MOR emissions. (2) Code contributors: earn MOR rewards based on weighted code contributions assessed by the community. ~24% of MOR emissions. (3) Compute providers: stake MOR and serve inference requests; earn MOR rewards proportional to compute provided. ~24% of MOR emissions. (4) Community/builders: ~24% for ecosystem development and builders. (5) Protection Fund: ~4% reserve. Total MOR rewards pool approximately $20M (varies with MOR price). No direct onboarding fee -- participation requires staking (compute/capital) or contribution (code). Gas fees on Arbitrum are minimal. MOR token has a fixed emission schedule declining over time. [uncertain]

governance_and_compliance.regulatory_alignment: unknown -- Morpheus is a permissionless, anonymous-team decentralized project with no corporate entity. No explicit regulatory framework compliance documented. The decentralized and pseudonymous nature may conflict with KYC/AML requirements in some jurisdictions. No EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, or ISO 42001 alignment. The project's philosophy prioritizes censorship resistance and permissionless access over regulatory compliance. [uncertain]
governance_and_compliance.audit_trail_capability: Blockchain anchoring (all MOR token transactions, staking events, capital deposits, and reward distributions are recorded immutably on Arbitrum/Ethereum; compute payment transactions provide a financial audit trail; code contributions are tracked on GitHub with transparent weighting). However, actual compute inference content (what the agent processes) is not recorded on-chain. Lumerin routing records may provide some session-level audit data but inference request/response content is transient. [uncertain]
governance_and_compliance.lifecycle_management: Registration (create wallet, acquire MOR tokens, stake or register with Lumerin) / activation (compute provider begins serving requests; Smart Agent connects to Lumerin routing) / suspension (unstake to stop receiving compute requests; stop running the agent process; no formal on-chain suspension mechanism) / upgrade (deploy updated Smart Agent code; no on-chain upgrade tracking; compute providers can update their hardware/models) / decommissioning (unstake MOR tokens, withdraw capital deposits, stop running agent; staking positions may have unbonding periods). No formal migration mechanism between wallet identities. The permissionless nature means agents can freely enter and exit. [uncertain]
